Contents

An introduction and seven chapters guiding you from the foundations of Open and Distance Learning to the responsible use of generative AI. Hover a chapter to preview it.

Preface

Introduction

W

elcome to your comprehensive guidebook for multimodal course design. As academics at the Open University of Sri Lanka (OUSL), we hold a unique and profound responsibility. We are not just lecturers standing in front of a classroom; we are the facilitators of accessible, flexible, and life-changing education. In our Open and Distance Learning (ODL) system, the materials we create are the very heart of the teaching-learning process. They serve as the teacher in the absence of a physical classroom, guiding, motivating, and inspiring our learners wherever they are.

For years, our trusted manual ‘Bridging the Gap’ served us well, providing a foundation for creating high-quality, print-based instructional materials. Yet, rapid and vast developments in technology have opened up newer avenues for delivering education, and today, our learners gain knowledge, skills, and attitudes through a vibrant mix of reading, listening, watching, and interactive clicking.

To meet these evolving needs, the Centre for Educational Technology and Media (CETMe) has developed this hands-on, multimodal guidebook. Consider this an expansion of our horizons; a user-friendly companion designed for you to refer to whenever you create or update your learning resources.

What’s inside this guide?

We have structured this guidebook to be as practical and accessible as possible, mapping out all modes of ODL delivery used at the OUSL. Accordingly, you will explore:

  • The Core of ODL: Grounding yourselves in the essential philosophies of distance education.
  • Diverse Modes of Delivery: Understanding how to seamlessly blend different educational channels.
  • The Design & Development Process: A step-by-step approach to building structured, cohesive courses.
  • Print-Based Materials: Refining your traditional, print-based instructional materials.
  • Audio-Visual Materials: Crafting engaging multimedia that brings complex concepts to life.
  • LMS-Based Learning: Maximising your Learning Management System to create vibrant, interactive online spaces.
  • Integrating Generative AI: Navigating and leveraging cutting-edge AI tools responsibly to enhance your workflow and enrich learning experiences.

How to use this book

This is not a theoretical textbook to be read once and shelved. It is a living, working toolkit. Keep bookmarked on your screen. Dive into specific chapters when you are stuck on a module design, use the checklists before deploying a new LMS quiz, or flip to the multimedia section when scripting your next educational video.

As OUSL academics, our mission is to break down barriers in education. By embracing the diverse, media-rich strategies within these pages, we ensure our courses remain engaging, inclusive, and world-class. Let’s dive in and design the future of learning together!

Chapter One

Open and Distance Learning

Dr. Sandhya Doluweera

Distance education has travelled a long journey, continuously improving as a method of teaching and learning. Distance learners have neither a regular classroom nor teachers to create an environment for learning; instructional materials take on that role.

At OUSL, self-learning materials are the main medium of instruction. The teacher is, in effect, built into the material, performing the tasks a classroom teacher would: presenting content, creating interaction, and motivating the learner. Because learners study at their own pace and place, course development is a rigorous, systematic process.

Self-learning materials, in essence
  • Written primarily for the learner, not the teacher
  • Always state aims and learning outcomes
  • Designed for a particular learner group
  • Place major emphasis on self-assessment

Self-learning vs. conventional materials

TextbooksSelf-Learning Materials
Assume interestArouse interest
Written for teacher useWritten for learner use
Do not indicate study timeGive estimates of study time
Rarely state aimsAlways give aims and outcomes
Little self-assessmentMajor emphasis on self-assessment

Source: IGNOU Handbook, Development and Revision of Self-Learning Material.

Chapter Two

Diverse Modes of ODL Delivery

Ms. Dinithi Ranasinghe

As an academic in the ODL system, you are not simply delivering content; you are designing learning experiences for students who study at a distance, often independently. Print-based Self-Instructional Materials remain the foundation, but effective delivery now draws on several modes together.

Used in combination
  • Print SIMs — the core, a “teacher in print”
  • Audio — useful on the move and for auditory learners
  • Video — to demonstrate and create teacher presence
  • LMS (Moodle) — resources, quizzes, and discussion
  • Multimodal materials — text, visuals, audio, activity integrated
  • Open Educational Resources — to enrich and save time

The key idea is integration, not addition: a lesson might introduce a concept in text, demonstrate it with a short video, and reinforce it with an activity, rather than posting a PDF, a video, and a quiz with no connection between them.

Key message
  • No single delivery mode works for everyone
  • Effective ODL programmes combine modes, tailored to learners and outcomes
Chapter Three

Design & Development Process

Ms. Geetha Kulasekara

The design and development of instructional materials follows a structured, iterative process, moving from broad outline to finished unit, with feedback gathered at each stage.

The development cycle
  • Identify the needs of the target group
  • Clarify aims and learning outcomes
  • Develop a framework for the course
  • Develop the study sessions
  • Compile the sessions into a unit

Role of the educational technologist

A professional dedicated to improving teaching and learning, who assists authors in strengthening a course — advising on structure, outcomes, media, activities, self-assessment, and evaluation.

Chapter Four

Print-Based Instructional Materials

Ms. Mayanthi Jayakody

The foremost service expected of an ODL academic is designing self-instructional materials (SIMs). A SIM is not a textbook; it is a carefully designed resource that replaces the physical presence of the teacher.

The role of the SIM

Classroom teacherThe SIM
Presents contentDelivers clear, structured explanations
Guides learningDirects the learner with objectives and activities
Gives feedbackOffers immediate feedback and model answers
Creates a friendly atmosphereAdopts a conversational tone (uses “you”)
A good OUSL SIM is
  • Self-pacing — progress at the learner’s own speed
  • Self-contained — all that the unit’s outcomes require
  • Active — embedded questions, tasks, reflections
  • Clear — plain language, local examples
  • Well signposted — headings, lists, margin notes

ODL rests on two pillars: the “Open” philosophy (open entry, place, and pace) and the “Distance” reality (teacher and learner separated in space or time). An ODL learner is typically an adult juggling work, family, and study, often studying in isolation.

Writing to the distance learner

Direct addressing
  • Use the second person (“you”), not “the learner”
  • Use the active voice, not the passive
  • Keep a conversational, warm tone
  • Ask questions and give directions

The house standard is British English; avoid over-capitalisation, and let punctuation act as a “silent tutor” for readers working in a second or third language.

Chapter Five

Audio-Visual Instructional Materials

Dr. Sameera Tilakawardena

ODL learners study at different times and places, with limited teacher contact. Audio-visual materials bridge this distance — pairing voice with visuals, demonstrating tasks, and supporting different learning preferences.

What makes AV “educational”
  • A clear learning outcome
  • Short and focused (3–7 minutes)
  • Visuals for meaning, not decoration
  • Good pacing and narration
  • Accessible to all learners

Types of audio-visual material

TypeBest for
Explainer videoConcepts and theories
DemonstrationPractical skills
Animated videoProcesses and abstract ideas
Screen recordingSoftware skills, LMS navigation
Microlearning clipOne skill at a time

The production process

Six stages
  • Define goals
  • Plan (script & storyboard)
  • Record
  • Edit
  • Review
  • Share
Common mistakes to avoid
  • Videos longer than ten minutes
  • Reading slides word-for-word
  • Poor audio
  • Too many animations
  • No clear objective
Chapter Six

LMS-Based Instructional Materials

Ms. Geetha Kulasekara

Online learning takes place over the internet, where students access lessons, videos, assignments, and activities from anywhere — live or self-paced — usually through a learning management system such as Moodle.

Designing the course
  • Align outcomes with assessments and activities
  • Structure by week or topic, with consistent templates
  • Chunk content into learning objects
A typical structure
  • Welcome and orientation
  • Weekly overviews
  • Learning materials
  • Activities
  • Assessments
  • Support and help

Engagement depends on instructor presence (announcements, feedback, live Q&A), interactive and collaborative activities, and good learner support. Quality assurance means reviewing against standards, gathering feedback, and reflecting for improvement.

Chapter Seven

Integrating Generative AI in Course Materials

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham
Page 1 of 15

Learning Outcomes & Overview

1. Learning Outcomes

On completing this chapter, you should be able to:

  • explain the role of generative AI in developing ODL course materials, together with its benefits and its limitations;
  • apply prompt engineering techniques, including role, contextual, few-shot, iterative, and chained prompting, to generate course content;
  • use generative AI to plan lessons and to draft explanations, worked examples, case studies, and learner activities;
  • generate formative assessment items and supporting visual or multimedia resources with the assistance of generative AI;
  • select an appropriate generative AI tool for a given stage of course development;
  • critically review AI-generated content for academic accuracy, pedagogical appropriateness, and alignment with learning outcomes; and
  • apply institutional quality assurance principles when incorporating AI-generated material into official course materials.

2. Overview

The development of high quality course materials is central to teaching and learning in Open and Distance Learning, where learners rely on these materials to build knowledge and skills independently. Generative AI, artificial intelligence capable of producing text, images, and other content from a prompt, gives academics a new way to support this work: drafting explanations, generating examples, designing activities, and improving the clarity of written material, so a lesson no longer has to start from a blank page.

Its role is to assist, not replace. The academic still decides the learning outcomes, the content, and whether the finished material meets institutional standards, while AI takes on the more repetitive writing tasks and offers alternative ways of presenting a concept. It is also adaptable: the same topic explained for a first-year undergraduate will read very differently from one written for a postgraduate audience, and the more context you give it, the closer the output gets to something usable.

None of this removes the need for review. AI-generated material should always be treated as a first draft: it can state something wrong with confidence, miss an important concept, or reach for an example that doesn't fit the context, so every output needs checking before it becomes part of the official course material. Used this way, across planning, explanations, examples, activities, and assessment, it becomes a genuine writing partner rather than a shortcut around good instructional design.

Watch: AI for Educators: Foundations

An open introduction to generative AI in education, a good starting point if this is new territory for you.

1 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 2 of 15

Key Concepts

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter that are specific to working with generative AI.

Generative Artificial Intelligence

An AI system that produces new text, images, presentations, or other content in response to user instructions, by drawing on patterns learned from large collections of information.

Prompt

The instruction given to a generative AI system describing the task, the intended audience, the educational context, and the expected format of the response.

Prompt Engineering

The practice of designing prompts, deliberately and often iteratively, so that a generative AI system produces accurate, relevant, and educationally meaningful output.

Iterative Prompting

A technique in which a lesson is built up through a sequence of smaller prompts, each refining or extending the previous output, rather than requesting the whole lesson at once.

Prompt Chaining

An extension of iterative prompting in which the output of one prompt is deliberately fed into the next, so that outline, explanation, examples, and activities are developed as a connected sequence.

Academic Validation

The process by which an academic reviews AI-generated content for technical accuracy, pedagogical appropriateness, and alignment with the curriculum before it is used in official course materials.

AI Hallucination

An instance in which a generative AI system produces information, a reference, or a claim that sounds plausible and is stated with confidence, but is inaccurate or fabricated.

2 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 3 of 15

4.1 Planning Course Materials

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

Developing effective ODL course materials involves more than presenting information. Learners studying independently need to apply concepts, reflect on their understanding, and connect theory to practice, so worked examples, activities, case studies, and assessments are essential parts of every lesson. Generative AI is particularly good at producing a wide range of these from a single prompt, a full set of examples, activities, and self-assessment questions rather than just an explanation, which helps academics build a complete, consistent learning unit more efficiently.

Planning is one of the most important stages in developing ODL course materials: a well-sequenced lesson gives learners a clear pathway, with each section building toward the intended outcomes. Generative AI can help organise content, suggest alternative sequences, such as a problem-based or application-oriented structure instead of a purely linear one, and identify prerequisite knowledge a learner needs before tackling more advanced material. A prompt about computer networks, for instance, might come back recommending a real-world scenario before the technical detail, a structure worth considering even if you don't take it exactly as written.

It can also help refine learning outcomes, suggesting measurable action verbs and checking that outcomes stay achievable within the scope of the lesson, and flag prerequisite concepts, such as reviewing data tables and primary keys before teaching database normalisation, that reduce frustration for learners with uneven backgrounds. As always, these suggestions need checking against the actual curriculum and cohort.

Lesson outline prompt
You are an instructional designer at the Open University of Sri Lanka. Develop a lesson for first-year undergraduate students studying through Open and Distance Learning. The lesson should include an introduction, clearly stated learning outcomes, learner-friendly explanations, worked examples, reflection questions, self-assessment activities, and a concise summary. Use clear academic language suitable for independent learners.
Planning activity

Select one lesson that you are currently developing or planning to revise. Using a Generative AI tool of your choice, generate a lesson outline that includes an introduction, learning outcomes, the main topics to be covered, suggested learner activities, and self-assessment questions. Compare the AI-generated outline with your existing lesson plan and identify areas where the proposed structure could be improved. Consider whether the sequence of topics is appropriate, whether additional learner support is required, and whether the suggested activities align with the intended learning outcomes. Revise the outline where necessary before proceeding to the next stage of course development.

3 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 4 of 15

4.2 Developing Learning Content

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

Once the lesson structure is set, Generative AI can help develop the content itself. Its biggest strength here is adapting explanations to the audience: specify the learner profile, level of study, and outcome, and the same concept can be pitched very differently for a postgraduate versus a first-year undergraduate, without losing accuracy.

It is especially useful against writer's block: ask for a few alternative introductions, summaries, or transitions and adapt whichever fits best, rather than starting from nothing. The results depend heavily on the prompt though. A general request produces a generic answer, so specify the subject, audience, style, length, and any instructional elements you want included.

It is also good at generating examples and analogies that make abstract ideas concrete: drawing on education, healthcare, banking, or engineering contexts for examples, or comparing computer memory to human memory for an analogy. Adapt examples to reflect local industries and practices where you can, and check analogies carefully, a neat comparison that is slightly wrong can plant a misconception rather than clear one up.

The same tool can tighten existing material that has grown lengthy or repetitive over several revisions, simplifying language and restructuring while preserving the original meaning. As with everything else in this chapter, treat the result as a draft: check technical accuracy, confirm it still matches the learning outcomes, and revise the language before it goes anywhere near a student.

Content development prompt
You are an experienced instructional designer developing course materials for undergraduate students studying through Open and Distance Learning. Prepare a lesson on Database Normalisation suitable for first-year learners with limited prior knowledge. The lesson should include an engaging introduction, measurable learning outcomes, learner-friendly explanations, two worked examples, one real-world case study, a reflection activity, three self-assessment questions with answers, and a concise summary. Use clear academic language suitable for independent learning.
Good practice

When requesting educational resources from Generative AI, ask the system to generate learning experiences rather than content alone. Instead of requesting only explanations, encourage the AI to produce examples, activities, reflection questions, assessment tasks, and opportunities for learner interaction. This approach results in more engaging ODL course materials that actively support independent learning rather than simply presenting information.

4 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 5 of 15

4.3 Developing Worked Examples

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

Worked examples play an important role in helping learners understand how theoretical concepts are applied in practice. In ODL environments, where learners often study independently, well-developed examples reduce cognitive load by demonstrating the reasoning process before learners attempt similar problems on their own. Generative AI can assist academics by generating multiple examples based on different contexts, allowing the most appropriate example to be selected and refined.

For example, an academic preparing a lesson on database normalisation could request examples from a university student registration system, a hospital information system, a banking application, or an online shopping platform. Reviewing several examples allows the academic to select the one that best supports the learning outcomes while adapting it to suit the local educational context.

Worked example prompt
Develop a worked example demonstrating Second Normal Form (2NF) using a university student registration database. Explain each step clearly, identify partial dependencies, and show how the tables are transformed into Second Normal Form. The explanation should be suitable for first-year undergraduate students studying independently.
5 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 6 of 15

4.4 Developing Case Studies

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

Case studies encourage learners to apply theoretical knowledge in realistic situations. Unlike worked examples, which demonstrate how a problem is solved, case studies require learners to analyse a situation, evaluate alternative solutions, and justify their decisions. Generative AI can assist academics by generating authentic scenarios based on professional practice across a range of disciplines.

For example, an education course might require learners to analyse the implementation of technology-enhanced learning within a rural school. A management course may examine organisational change within a public sector institution, while an engineering course could present a case involving project management challenges. These scenarios provide opportunities for learners to apply concepts while developing critical thinking and decision-making skills.

Although AI can generate realistic case studies, academics should review the scenarios carefully to ensure that they reflect local professional practices and remain appropriate for the intended learners.

6 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 7 of 15

4.5 Designing Learner Activities

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

Learning activities encourage learners to interact actively with course materials rather than simply reading information. Generative AI can support the development of a wide range of activities that promote independent learning and continuous engagement throughout a lesson.

For example, AI can generate reflection activities that encourage learners to relate theoretical concepts to their own professional experiences. It can produce concept-mapping exercises that help learners visualise relationships between ideas, problem-solving activities that require learners to apply newly acquired knowledge, and discussion questions suitable for online learning environments. Academics can also request activities based on Bloom's Taxonomy to ensure that learners progressively develop higher-order thinking skills.

Instead of placing all activities at the end of a lesson, academics should consider distributing shorter activities throughout the content. These activities provide learners with regular opportunities to pause, reflect, and consolidate their understanding before progressing to more advanced concepts.

Learner activity prompt
Generate three learner-centred activities for a lesson on cybersecurity. Include one reflection activity, one problem-solving activity, and one collaborative online discussion task suitable for undergraduate students studying through Open and Distance Learning.
7 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 8 of 15

4.6 Developing Formative Assessments

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

Self-assessment is an important feature of ODL because it enables learners to monitor their own progress and identify areas requiring additional study. Generative AI can assist academics by generating a variety of assessment formats, including multiple-choice questions, true or false questions, matching exercises, short-answer questions, and scenario-based problems.

One of the strengths of AI is its ability to generate explanations that accompany assessment questions. Instead of simply indicating whether an answer is correct, AI can produce feedback explaining why the correct answer is appropriate and why alternative responses are incorrect. This immediate feedback helps reinforce learning and supports independent study.

Despite these advantages, assessment questions should always be reviewed carefully. Academics should verify technical accuracy, ensure that only one option is clearly correct where appropriate, and confirm that each question measures the intended learning outcome rather than simple memorisation.

8 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 9 of 15

4.7 Visual and Multimedia Resources

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

Visual resources often improve learner understanding by presenting complex information in a more accessible form. While text remains the primary component of ODL course materials, diagrams, flowcharts, concept maps, timelines, tables, and infographics can significantly enhance the learning experience. Generative AI can assist academics in planning these visual resources by suggesting appropriate diagrams, generating descriptions that can be converted into illustrations, or recommending suitable visual representations for particular concepts.

Several AI-powered applications now enable academics to transform text descriptions into visual learning resources. For example, Napkin AI can convert written explanations into simple concept diagrams, while Canva Magic Design and Gamma can generate presentation slides and visual layouts from text prompts. These tools should be viewed as design assistants that help academics communicate information more effectively rather than replacing sound instructional design principles.

The same principle extends to multimedia resources. Many ODL programmes now incorporate multimedia alongside printed or digital course materials, and Generative AI can support this process by generating video scripts, narration for educational videos, podcast outlines, presentation notes, and storyboard ideas. Academics preparing multimedia resources may also use AI to produce interview questions, demonstration scripts, or summaries that accompany recorded lectures.

For example, after completing a lesson on network security, an academic could ask the AI to prepare a five-minute video script summarising the key concepts introduced in the lesson. The generated script can then be adapted and recorded as a supplementary learning resource for students.

9 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 10 of 15

4.8 Prompt Engineering

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

The quality of AI-generated content depends largely on the quality of the prompt behind it. A carefully constructed prompt guides the AI toward the task, audience, and format you actually need, whether that is a full lesson outline, a worked example, a set of learner activities, or self-assessment questions, and cuts down the editing required afterwards.

Effective prompts generally combine a few elements: the role the AI should adopt, information about the learners, the educational context, the learning outcomes, the expected writing style, and the format of the output. The four techniques below cover the most useful ways of putting these together.

Watch: Mastering Prompt Engineering for Teachers

A walkthrough of building strong prompts, covering much the same ground as the techniques on this page.

Role Prompting

Assign the AI a specific identity, such as an instructional designer or subject matter expert, before requesting content. This nudges it toward an educational register rather than a generic explanation. For example, an academic developing a lesson on programming might begin with:

Prompt
You are an experienced instructional designer at the Open University of Sri Lanka. Develop a lesson on Object-Oriented Programming for first-year undergraduate students studying through Open and Distance Learning. The lesson should include an introduction, measurable learning outcomes, learner-friendly explanations, worked examples, learner activities, self-assessment questions, and a summary.

Contextual Prompting

Supply detail about the learners and the setting before requesting content, rather than assuming the AI understands the audience: prior knowledge, course level, expected duration, and any institutional requirements. The more specific the brief, the less revision the output usually needs. For example:

Prompt
Prepare a lesson on cloud computing for first-year undergraduate students studying through Open and Distance Learning. Assume that learners have basic computer literacy but no previous knowledge of cloud technologies. Explain concepts using simple language, practical examples, short paragraphs, and frequent opportunities for learner reflection.

Few-Shot Prompting

Show the AI one or two examples of the expected format rather than describing it. This is especially useful when several academics are contributing to the same course and need to match an existing style. For instance:

Prompt
The following lesson illustrates the structure used throughout this course. Analyse its organisation and writing style, then develop a new lesson on Relational Database Management Systems using the same structure, level of detail, and writing style.

Iterative Prompting

Build a lesson through a series of smaller prompts rather than one large request, refining and expanding at each stage. A typical sequence:

  • Step 1: Generate the lesson outline.
  • Step 2: Expand each lesson section.
  • Step 3: Develop worked examples.
  • Step 4: Generate learner activities.
  • Step 5: Create formative assessment questions.
  • Step 6: Write the lesson summary.
  • Step 7: Review the lesson for clarity and consistency.

Prompt Chaining

Prompt chaining extends this by feeding the output of one prompt directly into the next: an outline becomes the input for detailed explanations, which become the basis for examples, then activities and assessments. It mirrors the natural process of course writing and tends to produce more coherent material than requesting everything at once.

Prompt engineering activity

Choose one lesson that you are currently preparing and develop it using each of the prompting techniques discussed in this section. Begin by creating a simple prompt and then progressively improve it using role prompting, contextual prompting, few-shot prompting, and iterative prompting. Compare the outputs generated at each stage by considering the quality of the explanations, the organisation of the lesson, the relevance of the examples, and the suitability of the learner activities. Reflect on which prompting technique produced the most useful results and identify situations where each technique may be most appropriate during course development.

Good practice

Effective prompt engineering is an iterative process rather than a single interaction. Academics should expect to refine prompts several times before obtaining outputs that are suitable for inclusion in course materials. Providing clear instructions, sufficient educational context, and specific expectations generally produces higher-quality content and reduces the amount of editing required.

Table 7.1: Selecting Prompting Techniques for Different Course Development Tasks

Course Development TaskRecommended TechniqueWhy It Is Effective
Planning a lessonContextual PromptingGenerates an organised lesson structure aligned with learner needs.
Writing learning outcomesRole PromptingProduces measurable outcomes using an instructional design perspective.
Developing lesson contentContextual + Iterative PromptingSupports learner-centred explanations that can be refined progressively.
Creating worked examplesRole PromptingProduces discipline-specific examples appropriate for the intended audience.
Developing learner activitiesContextual PromptingAligns activities with learning outcomes and learner characteristics.
Creating self-assessment questionsIterative PromptingEnables progressive improvement of question quality and feedback.
Revising existing materialsFew-Shot PromptingMaintains consistency with the style and structure of existing course materials.
Preparing PowerPoint presentationsPrompt ChainingConverts lesson content into structured presentation slides.
Developing Moodle quizzesPrompt ChainingGenerates questions, answers, feedback, and question banks systematically.
10 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 11 of 15

4.9 Using Generative AI Tools

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

The rapid development of Generative Artificial Intelligence has resulted in a wide range of tools that support different aspects of teaching, learning, and course development. Although many AI applications appear similar, they differ considerably in their strengths, capabilities, and intended use. Some are particularly effective for developing lesson content, while others specialise in generating presentations, creating diagrams, analysing documents, producing multimedia resources, or supporting assessment design. Understanding these differences enables academics to select the most appropriate tool for a particular stage of course development rather than relying on a single application for every task.

When selecting a Generative AI tool, academics should consider several factors, including the type of educational resource being developed, the quality of the generated output, ease of use, data privacy, and compatibility with existing teaching practices. It is also important to remember that no single AI system performs equally well across all educational tasks. Combining several complementary tools often produces better results than depending on one application alone. The table below summarises several widely used AI tools that can support academics during the development of ODL course materials.

Table 7.2: Recommended Generative AI Tools for ODL Course Development

AI ToolPrimary UseApplication in ODL Course DevelopmentFree Version
ChatGPTGeneral content generationDevelop lesson content, learning outcomes, learner activities, assessment questions, summaries, feedback, and study guides.Yes
ClaudeLong-form academic writingDraft and edit comprehensive course materials, improve readability, and review lesson content.Yes
Google GeminiEducational content and Google Workspace integrationGenerate learning materials, create quizzes, summarise documents, and support collaborative course development.Yes
Microsoft CopilotMicrosoft 365 integrationDevelop teaching materials, lesson plans, PowerPoint presentations, and instructional documents within Microsoft applications.Yes
NotebookLMDocument analysisAnalyse uploaded course materials, produce summaries, generate FAQs, create study guides, and develop revision resources.Yes
MagicSchool AIAI for educatorsGenerate lesson plans, rubrics, assessments, classroom activities, and differentiated learning resources.Yes
GammaPresentation developmentTransform lesson content into visually appealing presentations for online teaching or learner support.Yes
Canva Magic StudioVisual resource developmentDesign infographics, worksheets, posters, social media graphics, and visual learning resources.Yes
Napkin AIConcept visualisationConvert written explanations into diagrams, concept maps, and visual summaries suitable for course materials.Yes
Perplexity AIAI-assisted researchRetrieve current information, locate references, and support literature exploration during course development.Yes
ElicitResearch supportSearch academic literature, identify relevant studies, and summarise research findings.Yes
ConsensusEvidence-based searchingLocate research evidence from peer-reviewed publications to support academic content development.Yes
Brisk TeachingTeaching supportGenerate quizzes, provide learner feedback, develop rubrics, and support online teaching activities.Yes
ElevenLabsAI voice generationProduce high-quality narration for educational videos, podcasts, and multimedia learning resources.Yes
DALL·E or Adobe FireflyAI image generationCreate original illustrations and visual resources to complement ODL course materials.Yes (limited)

Selecting the Appropriate AI Tool

Each application tends to suit a different stage. ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Microsoft Copilot work well early on for planning, drafting, and assessment design. NotebookLM comes into its own once you have documents to analyse or summarise. Canva Magic Studio, Napkin AI, and Gamma handle diagrams and presentations. Perplexity, Elicit, and Consensus are worth turning to for tracking down academic literature.

Combining a few of these, say ChatGPT for a first draft, Napkin AI for a diagram, and Gamma for the slides, usually produces richer material than relying on any one tool for everything.

Practical activity

Choose one lesson from a course that you are currently developing and identify three different Generative AI tools that could support the development of that lesson. Use one tool to prepare the lesson content, a second tool to generate learner activities and formative assessments, and a third tool to create a supporting visual resource or presentation. Compare the outputs generated by each application by considering the quality of the content, ease of use, accuracy, and suitability for independent learners. Reflect on the advantages and limitations of each tool and identify the combination that best supports your course development process.

Good practice

Generative AI tools should be selected according to the educational task rather than their popularity. Academics are encouraged to experiment with different applications, compare their outputs, and develop workflows that combine the strengths of multiple AI systems. Regardless of the tool used, every generated resource should be reviewed carefully to ensure that it is accurate, pedagogically appropriate, aligned with the learning outcomes, and suitable for inclusion in official ODL course materials.

11 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 12 of 15

Quality Assurance

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

Generative AI can accelerate the drafting of course materials considerably, but responsibility for their academic quality remains entirely with the academic. The guidance gathered in this section applies across every stage of course development described in this chapter.

Reviewing AI-generated Content

When requesting educational resources from Generative AI, ask the system to generate learning experiences rather than content alone. Instead of requesting only explanations, encourage the AI to produce examples, activities, reflection questions, assessment tasks, and opportunities for learner interaction. This approach results in more engaging ODL course materials that actively support independent learning rather than simply presenting information.

Despite these advantages, AI-generated content should always be regarded as a draft that requires careful academic review. Academics should verify technical accuracy, ensure alignment with the learning outcomes, confirm the suitability of examples, and revise the language where necessary to maintain consistency throughout the course.

Academic Integrity and Institutional Policies

Generative AI should be used to support academic writing, not replace it. The strongest course materials are produced when academics combine AI-generated drafts with their own subject expertise, teaching experience, and understanding of learner needs. Every lesson should be reviewed for academic accuracy, consistency, accessibility, and alignment with institutional quality standards before it is incorporated into the final course material.

Selecting and Using AI Tools Responsibly

Generative AI tools should be selected according to the educational task rather than their popularity. Academics are encouraged to experiment with different applications, compare their outputs, and develop workflows that combine the strengths of multiple AI systems. Regardless of the tool used, every generated resource should be reviewed carefully to ensure that it is accurate, pedagogically appropriate, aligned with the learning outcomes, and suitable for inclusion in official ODL course materials.

Verifying References and Guarding Against Hallucination

AI systems may occasionally generate inaccurate information, omit important concepts, or present examples that are not appropriate for the intended learning context. They may also produce references that require verification or include statements that are inconsistent with the curriculum. Every AI generated output should therefore be reviewed critically by the academic before it becomes part of the official course material.

Refining Your Prompts

Effective prompt engineering is an iterative process rather than a single interaction. Academics should expect to refine prompts several times before obtaining outputs that are suitable for inclusion in course materials. Providing clear instructions, sufficient educational context, and specific expectations generally produces higher-quality content and reduces the amount of editing required.

Quality checklist
  • Have all facts, figures, and technical claims been verified against a trusted source?
  • Do the learning outcomes, explanations, examples, and assessment items align with one another?
  • Are the examples and case studies appropriate for the intended learners, and localised where relevant?
  • Has every reference or citation produced by the AI been checked, since these can be fabricated?
  • Does the language, tone, and level of detail match the rest of the course material?
  • Has the content been reviewed against institutional quality standards before publication?
12 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 13 of 15

Reflection, Summary & Resources

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

8. Reflection

Consider the following questions as you think about integrating generative AI into your own course development practice.

  • Which stages of your current course development process take the most time, and could generative AI reasonably support them?
  • What would you personally never delegate to AI when writing a lesson, and why?
  • How will you build a review step into your workflow, so that every AI-generated draft is checked before it reaches a learner?
  • Which prompting technique from this chapter, role, contextual, few-shot, iterative, or chained, feels most natural to your own way of writing?
  • How might you adapt AI-generated examples or case studies so that they reflect the Sri Lankan and OUSL context for your learners?

9. Summary

Generative Artificial Intelligence has become an important resource for academics involved in the development of ODL course materials. When integrated thoughtfully into the course writing process, AI can assist in planning lessons, generating learner-centred explanations, developing worked examples and case studies, designing learner activities, preparing formative assessments, improving the clarity of existing materials, and creating supporting multimedia resources. Prompt engineering techniques enable academics to obtain more relevant and accurate outputs, while the thoughtful selection of AI tools allows different stages of course development to be completed more efficiently.

Although Generative AI can significantly reduce the time required to prepare educational resources, the responsibility for academic quality remains with the academic. AI-generated content should always be reviewed, validated, and refined to ensure accuracy, consistency, and alignment with the intended learning outcomes. By combining Generative AI with sound instructional design principles and professional judgement, academics can create engaging, learner-centred course materials that effectively support independent learning in Open and Distance Learning environments.

10. Recommended OER Resources

Academics interested in exploring the use of Generative AI in education, and in higher education and ODL course development more broadly, may find the following openly available resources useful.

Video Resources
  • OpenAI YouTube Channel
  • Google for Education
  • Microsoft Learn for Educators
  • DeepLearning.AI: Generative AI for Everyone
  • UNESCO YouTube Channel and Webinars on Artificial Intelligence and Education
Open Educational Resources
  • UNESCO: Guidance for Generative AI in Education and Research
  • Commonwealth of Learning (COL) OER Repository
  • OpenLearn by The Open University (UK)
  • OER Commons
  • MERLOT
  • OpenStax
  • MIT OpenCourseWare

Chapter Seven · Page 14 of 15

Appendix A: Prompt Templates

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

These ready-to-use templates can be copied, pasted, and customised by replacing the text in square brackets. Tap a template to expand it.

Use this prompt when planning a new lesson.

Prompt
You are an experienced instructional designer at the Open University of Sri Lanka. Develop a lesson outline for the course [Course Name] on the topic [Topic].
The lesson is intended for [learner level] studying through Open and Distance Learning.
The lesson should include:
• Lesson title
• Introduction
• Learning outcomes
• Prerequisite knowledge
• Main lesson sections
• Suggested examples
• Learner activities
• Self-assessment questions
• Lesson summary
Write using clear academic language suitable for independent learners.

Use this prompt when writing the main lesson.

Prompt
Explain the topic [Topic] for [learner level] students studying independently through Open and Distance Learning.
Use:
• simple academic language
• short paragraphs
• headings and subheadings
• practical examples
• analogies where appropriate
• reflection questions throughout the lesson
• a summary at the end
Ensure the content supports independent learning.
Prompt
Generate three worked examples for the topic [Topic].
Each example should:
• explain the problem
• solve it step by step
• explain the reasoning
• identify common mistakes
• conclude with a short learner exercise.
Prompt
Develop learner-centred activities for the lesson [Topic].
Include:
• one reflection activity
• one practical activity
• one case study
• one discussion activity
• one collaborative activity suitable for Moodle
Align all activities with the learning outcomes.
Prompt
Develop formative assessment questions for the lesson [Topic].
Include:
• five multiple-choice questions
• three short-answer questions
• one scenario-based question
• model answers
• explanations for every answer
Ensure the questions assess understanding rather than memorisation.
Prompt
Review the following lesson as an instructional designer.
Evaluate:
• accuracy
• organisation
• clarity
• readability
• learner engagement
• alignment with learning outcomes
• opportunities for learner interaction
Suggest improvements without changing the academic meaning.
Prompt
Rewrite the following lesson section for undergraduate students studying independently through Open and Distance Learning.
Improve:
• clarity
• readability
• logical flow
• transitions
• consistency
Maintain academic accuracy and preserve the original meaning.
Prompt
Based on the following lesson, recommend Moodle learning activities that would support learner engagement.
Suggest appropriate uses of:
• Forum
• Quiz
• Assignment
• H5P Interactive Content
• Lesson Activity
• Glossary
• Database Activity
• Workshop
Explain how each activity supports the intended learning outcomes.
Prompt
Convert the following lesson into a lecture presentation.
Produce:
• slide titles
• concise bullet points
• speaker notes
• suggested diagrams
• learner questions
• summary slide
The presentation should support a two-hour teaching session.
Prompt
Develop an assessment rubric for the following assignment.
Include:
• assessment criteria
• performance levels
• marks allocation
• descriptors for Excellent, Good, Satisfactory and Needs Improvement
Ensure alignment with the learning outcomes.
Prompt
Suggest visual learning resources for the lesson [Topic].
Recommend:
• concept maps
• flowcharts
• diagrams
• infographics
• timelines
• comparison tables
For each recommendation, explain how it supports learner understanding.

This is especially useful for OUSL.

Prompt
Adapt the following lesson for learners studying in Sri Lanka.
Replace generic international examples with examples from:
• Sri Lankan education
• government organisations
• healthcare
• agriculture
• engineering
• banking
• business
Ensure the examples remain culturally appropriate and educationally relevant.
14 / 15
Chapter Seven · Page 15 of 15

Appendix B: AI Workflows

Ms. Shalini Rajasingham

These workflows show, from start to finish, how generative AI can support academics through a complete task rather than a single prompt.

Workflow 1: Developing a New ODL Lesson

Learning Outcomes
↓
Lesson Outline
↓
Lesson Content
↓
Worked Examples
↓
Learning Activities
↓
Self-Assessment Questions
↓
Summary
↓
Review with AI
↓
Academic Review
↓
Final Course Material

Workflow 2: Revising an Existing Lesson

Existing Lesson
↓
Upload to AI
↓
Identify Weaknesses
↓
Rewrite Sections
↓
Improve Examples
↓
Generate Activities
↓
Generate Assessments
↓
Final Academic Review

Workflow 3: Creating a Complete Moodle Course

Lesson Guide
↓
AI Generates
↓
Quiz
↓
Forum Questions
↓
Assignment
↓
Rubric
↓
H5P Activities
↓
Course Summary
↓
Moodle Upload
15 / 15
Closing

Conclusion

This guidebook is intended to be a practical guide throughout the course design journey. This is for developing a new course, updating existing materials, or exploring new approaches to teaching and learning, the guidance, examples, and checklists can be revisited whenever needed and adapted to suit different teaching contexts and learner needs.

As OUSL academics, we all share the goal of making learning accessible, engaging, and meaningful. Every improvement, whether refining a printed lesson, creating an interactive online activity, producing an educational video, or thoughtfully integrating Generative AI, contributes to a richer learning experience for our learners. Together, let us continue to build on the OUSL tradition of quality Open and Distance Learning by creating learning experiences that inspire, support, and empower learners wherever they are.

Thank You for Reading

This concludes Bridging the Gap, Part II, prepared for the academic staff of The Open University of Sri Lanka.

Centre for Educational Technology & Media (CETMe) · 2026
